title: Test the Gateway Endpoint
content:
Create S3 bucket Navigate to S3 management console In the Bucket console, choose Create bucket In the Create bucket console Name the bucket: choose a name that hasn’t been given to any bucket globally (hint: lab number and your name) Leave other fields as they are (default) Scroll down and choose Create bucket Successfully create S3 bucket. Connect to EC2 with session manager For this workshop, you will use AWS Session Manager to access several EC2 instances. Session Manager is a fully managed AWS Systems Manager capability that allows you to manage your Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises virtual machines (VMs) through an interactive one-click browser-based shell. Session Manager provides secure and auditable instance management without the need to open inbound ports, maintain bastion hosts, or manage SSH keys.
First Cloud AI Journey Lab for indepth understanding of Session manager.
In the AWS Management Console, start typing Systems Manager in the quick search box and press Enter: From the Systems Manager menu, find Node Management in the left menu and click Session Manager: Click Start Session, and select the EC2 instance named Test-Gateway-Endpoint. This EC2 instance is already running in “VPC Cloud” and will be used to test connectivity to Amazon S3 through the Gateway endpoint you just created (s3-gwe).
Session Manager will open a new browser tab with a shell prompt: sh-4.2 $
You have successfully start a session - connect to the EC2 instance in VPC cloud. In the next step, we will create a S3 bucket and a file in it.
Create a file and upload to s3 bucket Change to the ssm-user’s home directory by typing cd ~ in the CLI Create a new file to use for testing with the command fallocate -l 1G testfile.xyz, which will create a file of 1GB size named “testfile.xyz”. Upload file to S3 bucket with command aws s3 cp testfile.xyz s3://your-bucket-name. Replace your-bucket-name with the name of S3 bucket that you created earlier. You have successfully uploaded the file to your S3 bucket. You can now terminate the session.
Check object in S3 bucket Navigate to S3 console. Click the name of your s3 bucket In the Bucket console, you will see the file you have uploaded to your S3 bucket Section summary Congratulation on completing access to S3 from VPC. In this section, you created a Gateway endpoint for Amazon S3, and used the AWS CLI to upload an object. The upload worked because the Gateway endpoint allowed communication to S3, without needing an Internet Gateway attached to “VPC Cloud”. This demonstrates the functionality of the Gateway endpoint as a secure path to S3 without traversing the Public Internet.

title: Check cost after cleanup
content:
Items to check EC2 running instances Unattached EBS volumes Unused Elastic IPs RDS databases/snapshots S3 storage CloudFront distributions CloudWatch logs/alarms Lambda functions NAT Gateway if created Checking steps Open AWS Billing Dashboard. Check cost by service. Open Cost Explorer if detailed checking is needed. Check AWS Budgets alerts. Record cleanup results in the report. How to check remaining paid resources After deleting or stopping project resources, check the services that can still create cost. The goal is to confirm that the account no longer keeps unnecessary compute, database, storage, CDN, log, or networking resources.
aws ec2 describe-instances --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" --region ap-southeast-1 aws ec2 describe-volumes --filters "Name=status,Values=available" --region ap-southeast-1 aws ec2 describe-addresses --region ap-southeast-1 aws rds describe-db-instances --region ap-southeast-1 aws s3 ls aws cloudfront list-distributions aws lambda list-functions --region ap-southeast-1 If any resource is still required for the demo, keep it and record the reason. If it is no longer required, stop or delete it from the AWS Console.
Console checking steps Open AWS Billing Dashboard and check current month charges. Open Cost Explorer and group cost by service. Check whether EC2, RDS, S3, CloudFront, CloudWatch, Lambda, and NAT Gateway still appear. Open AWS Budgets and confirm that budget alerts are configured if the account will stay active. Take one screenshot for the report after cleanup. Simple cost report table Service Remaining resource Estimated status EC2 Running/stopped instance, EBS volume, Elastic IP Check and record RDS Database instance or snapshot Check and record S3 Media buckets and object storage Check and record CloudFront Distribution and data transfer Check and record CloudWatch Logs and alarms Check and record Lambda Remaining functions Check and record NAT Gateway Any active NAT Gateway Delete if not used Final cleanup conclusion In the report, conclude this section with a short sentence such as:
After cleanup, the main remaining cost risks are EC2/RDS if they are still running, S3 storage if media files are kept, and CloudFront data transfer if the distribution remains active. Check cost after the workshop Main cost sources:
EC2 running continuously. RDS instance and backup storage. S3 source videos and HLS outputs. MediaConvert charged by transcoding duration and output type. CloudFront data transfer when users watch videos. CloudWatch logs, metrics, and alarms. Cost Explorer CLI example aws ce get-cost-and-usage \ --time-period Start=2026-07-01,End=2026-07-31 \ --granularity MONTHLY \ --metrics UnblendedCost \ --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE Estimating cost for large movie uploads If 5 movies are uploaded and each source file is about 5GB, the cost mainly increases in these areas:
S3 input: about 25GB of source videos. S3 output: HLS multi-bitrate output can be close to or larger than the source size depending on settings. MediaConvert: charged by media duration and output resolution. CloudFront: charged by viewer traffic and segment downloads. Report table example Cost group Cause Optimization S3 Source videos and HLS output Delete test files, use lifecycle rules MediaConvert Multi-quality transcoding Output only needed qualities CloudFront Video streaming traffic Cache correctly, use CDN instead of EC2 RDS Database running 24/7 Use small instance during demo EC2 Server running 24/7 Stop when demo is not active

title: Configure CloudFront HLS streaming
content:
Goal CloudFront is used to distribute HLS streams from the S3 output bucket to viewers. The stream distribution is configured as protected HLS and uses signed cookies to restrict direct video access.
Stream distribution d11jdx7259stge.cloudfront.net The origin points to:
netflop-output-source.s3.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com Playback flow Frontend -> Backend retrieves episode info Backend -> issues stream session / signed cookies Video Player -> CloudFront -> S3 output HLS Related API The backend issues a stream session through:
/api/stream/session This API creates CloudFront signed cookies so the browser can download the HLS manifest and segments for a limited time. As a result, the frontend does not need to expose the raw HLS object URLs publicly.
Media proxy fallback The backend also provides a proxy API:
/api/media-proxy This endpoint handles cases where media or subtitle requests need proxying or when CORS/mixed content issues occur. In production, avoid exposing raw stream URLs in player error messages.
Verification Open the CloudFront console. Verify the distribution is Enabled. Verify the origin points to the S3 output bucket. Open an episode in Netflop. Confirm the player loads HLS segments through CloudFront. How signed cookies protect streams When a user opens an episode, the frontend requests a stream session from the backend. The backend creates CloudFront signed cookies and sets them in the response. The player then loads HLS through CloudFront.
Sample backend stream session async function createStreamSession(req, res, next) { const sourceUrl = req.body?.sourceUrl || req.query?.sourceUrl; const signedSession = cloudFrontSignedCookieService.buildSignedCookies(sourceUrl); cloudFrontSignedCookieService.setSignedCookies(res, signedSession); res.json({ success: true, data: { enabled: signedSession.enabled, playbackUrl: signedSession.playbackUrl, expiresAt: signedSession.expiresAt || null } }); } Sample cookie object return { enabled: true, playbackUrl, expiresAt, cookies: { 'CloudFront-Policy': toCloudFrontBase64(policy), 'CloudFront-Signature': signPolicy(policy), 'CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id': awsConfig.cloudFrontKeyPairId } }; Sample frontend player logic const useCredentialedPlayback = useMemo( () => shouldUseCredentialedPlayback(mediaUrl, signedPlaybackUrl), [mediaUrl, signedPlaybackUrl] ); const hls = new Hls({ xhrSetup: (xhr) => { xhr.withCredentials = useCredentialedPlayback; } }); CloudFront purpose CloudFront is placed in front of the S3 output bucket so users can stream HLS faster and more reliably. When stream protection is required, the backend issues signed cookies with a short TTL so the player can request the HLS manifest and segments.
Signed cookie flow The player prepares to play an HLS source. Frontend calls /api/stream/session with the source URL. Backend builds a policy that limits the HLS resource and expiration time. Backend signs the policy with the CloudFront private key. Browser receives CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id. Player plays the CloudFront URL and sends the cookies with requests. Backend stream session code async function createStreamSession(req, res, next) { const sourceUrl = req.body?.sourceUrl || req.query?.sourceUrl; const signedSession = cloudFrontSignedCookieService.buildSignedCookies(sourceUrl); cloudFrontSignedCookieService.setSignedCookies(res, signedSession); res.json({ success: true, data: { enabled: signedSession.enabled, playbackUrl: signedSession.playbackUrl, expiresAt: signedSession.expiresAt || null } }); } Cookie creation sample return { enabled: true, playbackUrl, expiresAt, cookies: { 'CloudFront-Policy': toCloudFrontBase64(policy), 'CloudFront-Signature': signPolicy(policy), 'CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id': awsConfig.cloudFrontKeyPairId } }; Player credentials sample const useCredentialedPlayback = shouldUseCredentialedPlayback(mediaUrl, signedPlaybackUrl); const hls = new Hls({ xhrSetup: (xhr) => { xhr.withCredentials = useCredentialedPlayback; } }); The player should not send credentials for non-signed public sources such as Cloudflare Stream, otherwise CORS can fail because wildcard origin is not valid with credentials.

title: Configure RDS and Security Group
content:
Goal Amazon RDS MySQL is used as the main database for Netflop. The production database is named:
web_xem_phim_final RDS information Attribute Value DB identifier netflop-db Engine MySQL Instance class db.t4g.micro Endpoint netflop-db.c76we6m8scy0.ap-southeast-1.rds.amazonaws.com Port 3306 Database web_xem_phim_final Importing the local database to RDS The local source dump is:
database/web_xem_phim_final_dump.sql Import procedure:
Export the local database to an SQL file. Clean any statements incompatible with RDS, such as DEFINER or triggers requiring SUPER. Create the web_xem_phim_final database on RDS. Import the dump into RDS. Verify the number of tables and movie/episode/user data. RDS Security Group In production, use:
Type Port Source MySQL/Aurora 3306 EC2 security group netflop-ec2-sg Do not leave RDS open to 0.0.0.0/0 long term. If importing from a local machine, allow the personal IP temporarily and close it after import.
Backend connection checks pm2 logs netflop-api curl https://netflop.win/api/catalog/genres curl https://netflop.win/api/movies?limit=12 If the API returns movie/genre data correctly, the backend has successfully connected to RDS.
How to create and import RDS Go to RDS -> Create database. Choose MySQL. Set DB identifier to netflop-db. Create the initial database as web_xem_phim_final. Restrict the RDS Security Group to port 3306 from the EC2 security group. Import the dump from local or from EC2. Sample import command mysql -h netflop-db.c76we6m8scy0.ap-southeast-1.rds.amazonaws.com \ -P 3306 \ -u <db-user> \ -p web_xem_phim_final < database/web_xem_phim_final_dump_rds.sql Sample backend RDS connection code const mysql = require('mysql2/promise'); const env = require('./env'); const pool = mysql.createPool({ host: env.db.host, port: env.db.port, database: env.db.database, user: env.db.user, password: env.db.password, waitForConnections: true, connectionLimit: env.db.connectionLimit, charset: 'utf8mb4', namedPlaceholders: true }); Connection verification curl https://netflop.win/api/movies?limit=12 curl https://netflop.win/api/catalog/genres If the API returns lists of movies and genres, the backend is reading data from RDS successfully.
Create RDS MySQL for Netflop The production database uses RDS MySQL. The database name is web_xem_phim_final. It stores users, movies, episodes, subtitles, watch history, comments, and ratings.
RDS setup and security steps Select MySQL engine. Choose a small instance for demo, for example db.t4g.micro. Set DB identifier to netflop-db. Create database web_xem_phim_final. Select the same VPC as EC2. Configure the RDS security group to allow port 3306 only from the EC2 security group. Do not open MySQL port 3306 to all public IPs in production. Import local database dump into RDS mysql -h netflop-db.xxxxxx.ap-southeast-1.rds.amazonaws.com \ -u admin -p web_xem_phim_final < database/web_xem_phim_final_dump.sql Backend RDS connection sample const pool = mysql.createPool({ host: env.db.host, port: env.db.port, database: env.db.database, user: env.db.user, password: env.db.password, waitForConnections: true, connectionLimit: env.db.connectionLimit, charset: 'utf8mb4', namedPlaceholders: true }); Post-import checks Backend starts without database errors. Homepage returns movie data. Sign in and sign up work. Admin can see movies and episodes.

title: Test admin video upload
content:
Upload flow Admin signs in to the admin page. Admin selects a movie/episode. Admin selects an MP4/MKV video file. Backend receives multipart upload request. Video is uploaded to netflop-input-source. Backend creates a MediaConvert job. MediaConvert writes HLS output to netflop-output-source. EventBridge receives COMPLETE. Lambda netflop-mediaconvert-notifier calls backend webhook. Backend updates episode upload_status to ready. Backend values to check jobId hls_url cloudfront_url upload_status How admin video upload works The admin UI uses multipart upload for large files. After upload completes, the backend creates an episode record and sends the source video to MediaConvert for HLS processing.
Sample frontend multipart upload call const payload = await uploadVideoInChunks({ file, movieId, episodeName, thumbnailUrl, duration, onProgress: setUploadProgress }); setUploadProgress(100); setMessage('Video uploaded to S3 and sent to MediaConvert for HLS processing.'); Sample upload API wrapper export const uploadApi = { startVideoMultipart: (payload) => axiosClient.post('/uploads/videos/multipart/start', payload), uploadVideoMultipartPart: (formData, options = {}) => axiosClient.post( '/uploads/videos/multipart/parts', formData, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data' }, onUploadProgress: options.onUploadProgress } ), completeVideoMultipart: (payload) => axiosClient.post('/uploads/videos/multipart/complete', payload) }; Sample backend after multipart complete const uploaded = await awsS3Service.completeVideoMultipartUpload({ key, uploadId, parts }); const pendingEpisode = await episodeModel.createUploadEpisode({ movieId, name: episodeName, sourceUrl: uploaded.s3Uri, uploadStatus: 'uploaded' }); const job = await mediaConvertService.createHlsJob({ inputS3Uri: uploaded.s3Uri, movieId, episodeId: pendingEpisode.MaTap }); Test scenario Select a movie and enter episode name. Select an MP4/MKV file. Watch upload progress. Confirm source file in S3 input. Confirm MediaConvert job changes from SUBMITTED/PROGRESSING to COMPLETE. Confirm episode status becomes ready. Open the user website and play the uploaded episode. Admin episode upload test This is the most important workshop feature because it combines frontend, backend, S3, MediaConvert, Lambda, and CloudFront.
UI test steps Sign in as administrator. Open episode management or the Video & Trailer step in add/edit movie. Enter movie ID, episode name, and optional episode banner. Select an MP4/MKV file. Click upload video. Watch the multipart upload progress bar. After upload finishes, the status changes to MediaConvert processing. When the job completes, the episode status becomes ready/completed. Open the watch page and play the new episode. Frontend chunk upload sample for (let offset = 0, partNumber = 1; offset < file.size; offset += CHUNK_SIZE, partNumber += 1) { const end = Math.min(offset + CHUNK_SIZE, file.size); const chunk = file.slice(offset, end); const formData = new FormData(); formData.append('key', uploadSession.key); formData.append('uploadId', uploadSession.uploadId); formData.append('partNumber', String(partNumber)); formData.append('chunk', chunk, file.name); const response = await uploadPartWithRetry(formData, { onUploadProgress: (event) => { const percent = ((uploadedBytes + event.loaded) / file.size) * 100; onProgress(Math.min(99, Math.max(1, percent))); } }); } Backend complete multipart and create job const uploaded = await awsS3Service.completeVideoMultipartUpload({ key, uploadId, parts }); const pendingEpisode = await episodeModel.createUploadEpisode({ movieId, name: episodeName, sourceUrl: uploaded.s3Uri, uploadStatus: 'uploaded' }); const job = await mediaConvertService.createHlsJob({ inputS3Uri: uploaded.s3Uri, movieId, episodeId: pendingEpisode.MaTap }); Why manual Sync is no longer required MediaConvert sends job state changes to EventBridge. Lambda netflop-mediaconvert-notifier receives the event and calls backend webhook /api/uploads/mediaconvert/events. The backend uses jobId/episodeId to update the episode status.
Lambda notifier sample const response = await fetch(webhookUrl, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'content-type': 'application/json', 'x-netflop-event-secret': secret }, body: JSON.stringify(event) });

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section lists and introduces the blogs our team posted while working on Netflop - a movie streaming website built on AWS.
Blog 1 - Why Our Team Chose AWS Elemental MediaConvert Instead of FFmpeg This blog explains why our team chose AWS Elemental MediaConvert instead of running FFmpeg on Amazon EC2 for video encoding. It focuses on reducing EC2 workload, automatically converting videos to multi-quality HLS, and making the media pipeline easier to scale.
Blog 2 - Why We Used Amazon CloudFront Instead of Serving Videos Directly from S3 This blog shares our experience using Amazon CloudFront to deliver HLS videos for a movie streaming website. It highlights benefits such as lower latency, caching at Edge Locations, fewer direct S3 requests, HTTPS support, and content protection with CloudFront Signed Cookies.
Blog 3 - Building an Event-Driven Media Pipeline on AWS This blog describes how our team used an Event-Driven Architecture with MediaConvert -> EventBridge -> Lambda -> Backend Webhook to automatically update episode status after encoding is completed, instead of making the Backend poll continuously. Facebook source: https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/?multi_permalinks=2218428612255432

Components to monitor Component Metrics/logs EC2 CPUUtilization, NetworkIn/Out, StatusCheckFailed PM2/backend error logs, API 500, API health check Nginx access log and error log RDS CPUUtilization, FreeStorageSpace, DatabaseConnections S3 bucket size and request count CloudFront 4xx/5xx error rate and data transfer Lambda Errors, Duration, Invocations MediaConvert Job ERROR/CANCELED Create SNS topic for alerts aws sns create-topic --name netflop-alerts aws sns subscribe --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:ap-southeast-1:<account-id>:netflop-alerts --protocol email --notification-endpoint <email> EC2 high CPU alarm example aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \ --alarm-name netflop-ec2-high-cpu \ --metric-name CPUUtilization \ --namespace AWS/EC2 \ --statistic Average \ --period 300 \ --threshold 80 \ --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \ --evaluation-periods 2 \ --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx Verification Open CloudWatch -> Alarms. title: Configure storage, database, and media processing
content:
This section focuses on the Netflop media pipeline. It is the most important part of the movie website because admins must upload large videos, the system automatically converts them to multi-quality HLS, stores output on S3, and serves playback through CloudFront.
Contents Configure S3 input/output buckets Configure MediaConvert Configure CloudFront HLS streaming Configure subtitle converter Lambda Storage and media pipeline This is the core part of the streaming website. The backend no longer stores large videos in local upload folders. Instead, it uses S3 and MediaConvert to produce HLS playback sources.
Complete media flow Admin selects an MP4/MKV file in the admin UI. Frontend uploads the file with multipart upload to support large videos. Backend receives chunks and uploads them to the S3 input bucket. After multipart completion, backend creates an episode record with processing status. Backend calls MediaConvert to create multi-quality HLS output. MediaConvert writes output into the S3 output bucket. EventBridge invokes Lambda when the job status changes. Lambda notifies the backend and the backend changes the episode status to ready. The player uses a CloudFront playback URL. title: Configure subtitle converter Lambda
Access S3 from on-premises
content:
Overview In this section, you will create an Interface endpoint to access Amazon S3 from a simulated on-premises environment. The Interface endpoint will allow you to route to Amazon S3 over a VPN connection from your simulated on-premises environment.
Why using Interface endpoint:
Gateway endpoints only work with resources running in the VPC where they are created. Interface endpoints work with resources running in VPC, and also resources running in on-premises environments. Connectivty from your on-premises environment to the cloud can be provided by AWS Site-to-Site VPN or AWS Direct Connect. Interface endpoints allow you to connect to services powered by AWS PrivateLink. These services include some AWS services, services hosted by other AWS customers and partners in their own VPCs (referred to as PrivateLink Endpoint Services), and supported AWS Marketplace Partner services. For this workshop, we will focus on connecting to Amazon S3.

title: On-premises DNS Simulation
content:
AWS PrivateLink endpoints have a fixed IP address in each Availability Zone where they are deployed, for the life of the endpoint (until it is deleted). These IP addresses are attached to Elastic Network Interfaces (ENIs). AWS recommends using DNS to resolve the IP addresses for endpoints so that downstream applications use the latest IP addresses when ENIs are added to new AZs, or deleted over time.
In this section, you will create a forwarding rule to send DNS resolution requests from a simulated on-premises environment to a Route 53 Private Hosted Zone. This section leverages the infrastructure deployed by CloudFormation in the Prepare the environment section.
Create DNS Alias Records for the Interface endpoint Navigate to the Route 53 management console (Hosted Zones section). The CloudFormation template you deployed in the Prepare the environment section created this Private Hosted Zone. Click on the name of the Private Hosted Zone, s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com: Create a new record in the Private Hosted Zone: Record name and record type keep default options Alias Button: Click to enable Route traffic to: Alias to VPC Endpoint Region: US East (N. Virginia) [us-east-1] Choose endpoint: Paste the Regional VPC Endpoint DNS name from your text editor (you saved when doing section 4.3) Click Add another record, and add a second record using the following values. Click Create records when finished to create both records. Record name: *. Record type: keep default value (type A) Alias Button: Click to enable Route traffic to: Alias to VPC Endpoint Region: US East (N. Virginia) [us-east-1] Choose endpoint: Paste the Regional VPC Endpoint DNS name from your text editor The new records appear in the Route 53 console:
Create a Resolver Forwarding Rule Route 53 Resolver Forwarding Rules allow you to forward DNS queries from your VPC to other sources for name resolution. Outside of a workshop environment, you might use this feature to forward DNS queries from your VPC to DNS servers running on-premises. In this section, you will simulate an on-premises conditional forwarder by creating a forwarding rule that forwards DNS queries for Amazon S3 to a Private Hosted Zone running in “VPC Cloud” in-order to resolve the PrivateLink interface endpoint regional DNS name.
From the Route 53 management console, click Inbound endpoints on the left side bar In the Inbound endpoints console, click the ID of the inbound endpoint Copy the two IP addresses listed to your text editor From the Route 53 menu, choose Resolver > Rules, and click Create rule: In the Create rule console: Name: myS3Rule Rule type: Forward Domain name: s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com VPC: VPC On-prem Outbound endpoint: VPCOnpremOutboundEndpoint Target IP Addresses: Enter both IP addresses from your text editor (inbound endpoint addresses) and then click Submit You have successfully created resolver forwarding rule.
Test the on-premises DNS Simulation Connect to Test-Interface-Endpoint EC2 instance with Session manager Test DNS resolution. The dig command will return the IP addresses assigned to the VPC Interface endpoint running in VPC Cloud (your IP’s will be different): dig +short s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com The IP addresses returned are the VPC endpoint IP addresses, NOT the Resolver IP addresses you pasted from your text editor. The IP addresses of the Resolver endpoint and the VPC endpoint look similar because they are all from the VPC Cloud CIDR block.
Navigate to the VPC menu (Endpoints section), select the S3 Interface endpoint. Click the Subnets tab and verify that the IP addresses returned by Dig match the VPC endpoint: Return to your shell and use the AWS CLI to test listing your S3 buckets: aws s3 ls --endpoint-url https://s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com Terminate your Session Manager session: In this section you created an Interface endpoint for Amazon S3. This endpoint can be reached from on-premises through Site-to-Site VPN or AWS Direct Connect. Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoints simulated forwarding DNS requests from on-premises to a Private Hosted Zone running the cloud. Route 53 inbound Endpoints recieved the resolution request and returned a response containing the IP addresses of the VPC interface endpoint. Using DNS to resolve the endpoint IP addresses provides high availability in-case of an Availability Zone outage.

title: Workshop
content:
Deploying the Netflop Movie Website on AWS This workshop describes the process of deploying the Netflop movie streaming website to AWS. The content follows the current system flow: users access the netflop.win domain, the frontend and backend run on EC2, data is stored in RDS MySQL, media is stored on S3, videos are transcoded with AWS Elemental MediaConvert, and playback is delivered through CloudFront.
The goal of this chapter is to present how to build a movie streaming system capable of uploading large videos, automatically converting them to HLS at multiple resolutions, delivering video through a CDN, storing watch history by account, supporting subtitles, and monitoring operational status with CloudWatch.
Contents Workshop overview Prerequisites Create AWS infrastructure Configure storage, database, and media processing Deploy and test the application Clean up resources Practical implementation scope This workshop section documents how the Netflop movie streaming website was moved from local development to AWS. The content follows a hands-on structure: infrastructure setup, application deployment, video processing, stream protection, subtitle handling, monitoring, and cleanup.
The sample code snippets are shortened from the current Netflop source code. Sensitive values such as database passwords, AWS access keys, OAuth client secrets, JWT secrets, and CloudFront private keys must not be copied into the report.
Recommended structure for each feature State the purpose of the feature. List the AWS services or application modules involved. Describe the implementation steps in AWS Console or CLI. Explain the processing flow in the source code. Include a short code sample from the project. Add test results and screenshots required for evidence. Main features covered in the workshop Group Implemented feature Evidence to include Infrastructure EC2, Nginx, PM2, RDS, Security Groups Website loads and API health check works Media storage S3 input/output, CloudFront Source video in input bucket, HLS in output bucket Video processing MediaConvert, EventBridge, Lambda notifier Job COMPLETE and episode status updates automatically Subtitles VTT/SRT upload, Lambda SRT to VTT converter Subtitle track is visible in the player Stream security CloudFront signed cookies HLS plays through CloudFront with temporary cookies Operations CloudWatch, SNS, Cost Explorer Alarm, logs, dashboard, or cost screenshots Demo Stream Open HLS demo stream

title: VPC Endpoint Policies
content:
When you create an interface or gateway endpoint, you can attach an endpoint policy to it that controls access to the service to which you are connecting. A VPC endpoint policy is an IAM resource policy that you attach to an endpoint. If you do not attach a policy when you create an endpoint, AWS attaches a default policy for you that allows full access to the service through the endpoint.
You can create a policy that restricts access to specific S3 buckets only. This is useful if you only want certain S3 Buckets to be accessible through the endpoint.
In this section you will create a VPC endpoint policy that restricts access to the S3 bucket specified in the VPC endpoint policy.
Connect to an EC2 instance and verify connectivity to S3 Start a new AWS Session Manager session on the instance named Test-Gateway-Endpoint. From the session, verify that you can list the contents of the bucket you created in Part 1: Access S3 from VPC: aws s3 ls s3://\<your-bucket-name\> The bucket contents include the two 1 GB files uploaded in earlier.
Create a new S3 bucket; follow the naming pattern you used in Part 1, but add a ‘-2’ to the name. Leave other fields as default and click create Successfully create bucket
Navigate to: Services > VPC > Endpoints, then select the Gateway VPC endpoint you created earlier. Click the Policy tab. Click Edit policy. The default policy allows access to all S3 Buckets through the VPC endpoint.
In Edit Policy console, copy & Paste the following policy, then replace yourbucketname-2 with your 2nd bucket name. This policy will allow access through the VPC endpoint to your new bucket, but not any other bucket in Amazon S3. Click Save to apply the policy. { "Id": "Policy1631305502445", "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "Stmt1631305501021", "Action": "s3:*", "Effect": "Allow", "Resource": [ "arn:aws:s3:::yourbucketname-2", "arn:aws:s3:::yourbucketname-2/*" ], "Principal": "*" } ] } Successfully customize policy
From your session on the Test-Gateway-Endpoint instance, test access to the S3 bucket you created in Part 1: Access S3 from VPC aws s3 ls s3://<yourbucketname> This command will return an error because access to this bucket is not permitted by your new VPC endpoint policy:
Return to your home directory on your EC2 instance cd~ Create a file fallocate -l 1G test-bucket2.xyz Copy file to 2nd bucket aws s3 cp test-bucket2.xyz s3://<your-2nd-bucket-name> This operation succeeds because it is permitted by the VPC endpoint policy.
Then we test access to the first bucket by copy the file to 1st bucket aws s3 cp test-bucket2.xyz s3://<your-1st-bucket-name> This command will return an error because access to this bucket is not permitted by your new VPC endpoint policy.
Part 3 Summary: In this section, you created a VPC endpoint policy for Amazon S3, and used the AWS CLI to test the policy. AWS CLI actions targeted to your original S3 bucket failed because you applied a policy that only allowed access to the second bucket you created. AWS CLI actions targeted for your second bucket succeeded because the policy allowed them. These policies can be useful in situations where you need to control access to resources through VPC endpoints.

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship in the Workforce Bootcamp - First Cloud AI Journey program, I had the opportunity to learn and practice topics related to AWS, Cloud Architecture, DevOps, and AI through a real project context: the Netflop movie streaming web system.
By studying Netflop’s current architecture, I improved my ability to understand system design, analyze the role of each AWS service, document the media processing flow, identify security risks, and organize technical information into a structured report. The main areas I worked on included EC2/Nginx, React frontend, Node.js backend API, RDS MySQL, S3, MediaConvert, CloudFront, Lambda, EventBridge, CloudWatch, SNS, IAM, and Systems Manager.
In terms of work attitude, I tried to complete the report content week by week, actively recorded new knowledge, compared technical information with the project notes, and revised the report so it matched the perspective of an internship student. Besides the results achieved, I also realized that I need to improve my technical depth, system testing skills, and ability to present information more concisely.
Self-Assessment Table No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 AWS fundamentals Understanding the role of EC2, RDS, S3, CloudFront, Lambda, EventBridge, CloudWatch, SNS, and IAM in a web architecture ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 System architecture analysis Ability to describe the User/Admin flow, backend API, database, and Netflop media pipeline ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Learning ability Proactively learning new services such as MediaConvert, CloudFront signed cookies, Lambda triggers, and MCP/AI Agents ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Documentation and reporting Ability to summarize technical information into worklog, proposal, event report, and self-assessment sections ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Security mindset Identifying important areas such as RDS public access, IAM roles, S3 policies, CloudFront protection, and monitoring ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Testing and operations Testing main flows such as video upload, HLS playback, subtitles, avatar upload, and monitoring, while still needing deeper practice ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Proactiveness Proactively reviewing content, studying architecture, and adjusting the report to match the project focus ✅ ☐ ☐ 8 Responsibility Completing the 12-week worklog and ensuring the main report sections are connected ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Communication and feedback Receiving feedback, revising content, and asking questions when requirements are unclear ☐ ✅ ☐ 10 Problem-solving mindset Breaking down technical problems, identifying causes, and proposing improvements ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Applying knowledge to a real project Connecting AWS/DevOps/AI knowledge from the program and events to the Netflop system ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall evaluation Completed the internship goals well and improved cloud thinking and technical reporting skills ✅ ☐ ☐ Achievements Understood how a real website is deployed on AWS instead of viewing each service separately. Described the Netflop architecture, including web layer, backend API, database, media storage, video processing, CDN, and automation. Understood the video upload flow from backend to S3, processing with MediaConvert, and HLS delivery through CloudFront. Understood how EventBridge and Lambda automate video processing status updates. Improved awareness of cloud security, especially IAM, security groups, S3 access, RDS access, and signed cookies. Practiced technical reporting, weekly worklog writing, and summarizing lessons learned from events. Strengths Strong self-learning attitude and willingness to read more documentation when facing new AWS services. Ability to connect theoretical knowledge with a real web architecture. Organized work through a clear 12-week worklog. Ability to identify system limitations and propose improvements instead of only describing results. Areas For Improvement Need more hands-on practice with production deployment, CI/CD, and real cloud troubleshooting. Need to improve log analysis, CloudWatch usage, and detailed testing checklist design. Need to present reports more concisely and avoid repeating content. Need to improve technical communication, especially when explaining architecture to people who are not familiar with the system. Future Direction After the internship, I want to continue learning more about AWS Solutions Architecture, DevOps automation, cloud security, and applying AI to system operations. I also want to practice more with CI/CD, Infrastructure as Code, observability, and safer deployment patterns for real web applications.

title: Clean up resources
content:
This section explains how to review and clean up AWS resources after the workshop to avoid unnecessary costs.
Contents Clean up application resources Clean up media and automation resources Check cost after cleanup Cleanup goal Cleanup reduces cost after a demo or when the environment is no longer used. Important data should be backed up before deleting resources.
Cleanup principles Back up the database before deleting RDS. Export or keep important videos before deleting S3 objects. Stop EC2 for temporary breaks; terminate only when no longer needed. Delete CloudFront last because it must be disabled before deletion. Check Cost Explorer after cleanup. Suggested cleanup order Back up RDS -> Stop PM2/Nginx if needed -> Empty S3 test prefixes -> Disable CloudFront test distribution -> Delete Lambda/EventBridge test rules -> Stop or terminate EC2 -> Delete RDS snapshot or database only if no longer needed

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
After participating in the First Cloud AI Journey program and completing my internship report on the AWS architecture of the Netflop system, I would like to share some feedback based on my learning, documentation work, and community event experience.
Overall Evaluation 1. Program content
The program helped me approach AWS through a structured learning path, from foundational services to more advanced topics such as DevOps, AI Agents, Amazon Q, and system security. Combining self-study, report writing, event participation, and real project analysis helped me understand the knowledge better than only reading documentation.
2. Suitability for interns
The content is suitable for Information Technology students because it includes both fundamentals and opportunities to apply knowledge to a project. While working on the Netflop report, I was able to connect AWS knowledge with a real system using EC2, RDS, S3, MediaConvert, CloudFront, Lambda, EventBridge, and CloudWatch.
3. Technical learning opportunities
During the internship, I learned how to look at a cloud system through multiple layers: application, database, storage, media processing, CDN, automation, monitoring, and security. This was valuable because it showed me that deploying a website is not only about writing code; it also requires operations, protection, observability, and optimization.
4. Community activities and events
The FCAJ Community Day - AI Agents and DevOps event was a memorable highlight. Sessions about AI Agents, Amazon Q, AWS DevOps Agent, Voice AI, and Secure Private MCP gave me a new perspective on the future of Cloud/DevOps. The event atmosphere was open and practical, with many real examples that motivated me to keep learning.
5. Support from mentors and the community
I appreciate the support from mentors and the FCAJ community. When I faced difficult topics, I could refer to materials, ask questions, and learn from how others explained technical concepts. This made me more confident while learning AWS and preparing the report.
What I Was Most Satisfied With What I appreciated most was the opportunity to study a real architecture instead of only completing a simple lab. By analyzing Netflop, I understood the role of each AWS service and how they work together to serve end users. In particular, the video processing flow using S3, MediaConvert, and CloudFront helped me see how AWS can be applied in a real movie streaming website.
Challenges Some services such as MediaConvert, CloudFront signed cookies, EventBridge, and Lambda triggers were new to me and required more time to understand correctly. Turning technical information into a clear report was challenging, especially when explaining both the overall architecture and detailed workflows. Security-related topics such as IAM, private access, RDS security groups, and Cognito required careful checking to avoid inaccurate descriptions. Suggestions For Improvement Provide more sample reports for different directions such as web applications, data pipelines, DevOps automation, or AI applications. Provide an end-of-program checklist for each report section: Worklog, Proposal, Blog, Event, Workshop, Self-evaluation, and Feedback. Add a session on drawing AWS Architecture Diagrams and explaining architecture through user flows. If possible, include a short mid-term review so students can confirm whether their report is going in the right direction. Expectations After The Program After the program, I hope to continue joining activities related to AWS, DevOps, AI Agents, and cloud security. I also want more hands-on practice with CI/CD, Infrastructure as Code, monitoring, and production deployment patterns to develop my Cloud Engineer skills.
Appreciation I would like to thank the First Cloud AI Journey program, mentors, and community for supporting my learning, practice, and internship report completion. The program helped me gain a clearer direction in Cloud/DevOps and understand the importance of combining learning with real system analysis.
